refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the verification boundary and prevents ambiguity in acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation communication path (operator input → vehicle → operator feedback) over sustained operational periods. Compare measured loss against customer-defined threshold.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator control commands with reliable delivery to the remote vehicle.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit vehicle telemetry and sensor data to the operator with reliable delivery.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss, latency) to the operator and safety systems.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement error recovery and retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) management with packet prioritization, compression, and selective retransmission for critical control/safety data.
DP-C: Integrate network monitoring and predictive congestion avoidance with dynamic bandwidth allocation to maintain packet loss below threshold.
DP-D: Combine redundant channels (DP-A) with adaptive QoS (DP-B) and network monitoring (DP-C) in a layered resilience architecture.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a layered teleoperation resilience architecture combining (1) redundant communication channels with automatic failover, (2) adaptive QoS management with packet prioritization and selective retransmission, and (3) real-time network monitoring with predictive congestion avoidance and dynamic bandwidth allocation.

potential_sources: Redundancy logic (DP-A) must coordinate with QoS prioritization (DP-B) to avoid conflicting bandwidth allocation decisions.
Network monitoring (DP-C) must feed state to both failover logic (DP-A) and QoS adaptation (DP-B) without creating circular dependencies.
Safety-critical control data must be prioritized consistently across all three layers to prevent priority inversion.
coupling_check.notes: Layered architecture decouples concerns: redundancy handles path-level failures, QoS handles congestion-level degradation, monitoring provides situational awareness. Coupling is manageable via clear interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert). High safety criticality requires deterministic behavior; recommend formal verification of failover and prioritization logic.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss threshold and network conditions are formally documented and stable.
ASSUMPTION: Dual network paths (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual cellular carriers) are available at the worksite.
RISK: Redundant channels increase system complexity and cost; cost-benefit trade-off must be validated with customer.
RISK: Adaptive QoS and dynamic bandwidth allocation introduce non-deterministic behavior; safety-critical control paths must be isolated from adaptive logic.
RISK: Network monitoring overhead (probes, telemetry collection) may itself consume bandwidth and worsen packet loss if not carefully bounded.
RISK: Failover latency (switching between channels) may exceed operator tolerance; requires testing and tuning.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly separate safety-critical control path (deterministic, high-priority, redundant) from best-effort telemetry path (adaptive QoS). Define clear SLAs for each path: control path target ≤0.1% loss, telemetry path target ≤1% loss. This clarifies the design intent and simplifies verification.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication pipeline for remote equipment operation, including operator input transmission, vehicle command reception, telemetry collection, and operator feedback delivery. Encompasses redundant channel management, QoS adaptation, network monitoring, and failover logic.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator control commands (joystick input, mode selection, task parameters) from the operator console or tele-operator interface.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and communication quality metrics (packet loss, latency, signal strength) to the operator console and monitoring systems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits communication degradation alerts and failover events to Safety Services for operator notification and emergency response coordination.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Manages dual network paths (primary and backup) for bidirectional communication; abstracts underlying network technology (cellular, satellite, wired).

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss mitigation logic. All DP-D components (redundancy, QoS, monitoring) are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface crosses to Safety Services for critical alerts.

Vehicle module owns the remote equipment and its sensors/actuators, but does not own communication resilience logic. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would scatter teleoperation logic across vehicle firmware and operator console, violating cohesion. Vehicle should only implement command reception and telemetry collection; Teleoperation handles transmission quality.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and emergency response, not communication resilience. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would conflate communication engineering with safety logic, creating tight coupling and diffusion. Safety Services should consume SafetyAlert from Teleoperation and act on it.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. While it could theoretically own DP-D, doing so would bypass the Teleoperation module's responsibility and create architectural ambiguity. DP-D should be owned by Teleoperation; NGHE Solution integrates Teleoperation with other subsystems.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (layered resilience architecture). It has clear responsibility for communication quality, owns the operator-vehicle communication pipeline, and can implement redundancy, QoS, and monitoring without diffusion. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become large and complex if it also owns low-level network protocol handling. Recommend internal decomposition (e.g., Redundancy Manager, QoS Adapter, Network Monitor subcomponents) to maintain cohesion.

RISK: Network Link interface abstraction must be robust; if underlying network technology changes (e.g., new cellular standard), Teleoperation must not require rework. Recommend versioning and backward compatibility in Network Link contract.
RISK: SafetyAlert interface must have guaranteed delivery and low latency; if Teleoperation is congested, alerts may be delayed. Recommend out-of-band safety alert channel or priority queue.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has sufficient engineering capacity and expertise to implement DP-D (redundancy, QoS, monitoring). If not, consider splitting Teleoperation into two modules: TeleopComm (communication resilience) and TeleopControl (operator interface and command sequencing). Ensure Network Link interface is abstracted to support multiple network technologies without Teleoperation rework.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (layered resilience architecture) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤1%). The three layers (redundancy, QoS, monitoring) are necessary and sufficient to achieve the acceptance criterion under agreed network conditions. No logical gaps or misalignments detected.

diffusion_risks: Redundancy failover logic must coordinate with Vehicle module's command reception to avoid race conditions during channel switching. Recommend explicit handshake protocol.
QoS prioritization must be coordinated with Safety Services' alert delivery to ensure safety-critical messages are never deprioritized. Recommend reserved bandwidth for SafetyAlert.
Network monitoring probes must not interfere with operational telemetry; recommend separate monitoring channel or time-division multiplexing.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module cleanly contains DP-D with minimal external dependencies. Network Link interface abstracts underlying network complexity. Tasking and Telemetry interfaces are standard data flows. SafetyAlert is the only cross-module dependency; recommend explicit SLA (latency, reliability) in interface contract.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Packet loss measurement is straightforward: inject test packets across teleoperation path, measure loss under various network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints). Recommend automated test harness with configurable network emulation. Verification can be performed in lab and field environments.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-009 is clearly allocated to Teleoperation module. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) can be further allocated to Teleoperation subcomponents (Redundancy Manager, QoS Adapter, Network Monitor, Error Recovery) in next decomposition level. Acceptance criterion (≤1% packet loss) is measurable and testable.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the Network Link interface contract to specify supported network technologies, failover latency bounds, and bandwidth guarantees.
Define explicit SLAs for SafetyAlert delivery: maximum latency, minimum reliability (e.g., 99.99% delivery), and priority relative to operational telemetry.
Establish a test plan for DP-D validation, including lab testing with network emulation and field testing under real worksite conditions.
Document assumptions about customer-defined packet loss threshold and network conditions; obtain formal sign-off from customer.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command transmission, telemetry transmission, quality reporting, error recovery). DP-D is sound and well-aligned with the refined FR. Teleoperation module is the correct owner with minimal diffusion. However, DP-D is complex (three layers: redundancy, QoS, monitoring) and requires further decomposition to clarify implementation details, interface contracts, and verification strategy. Recommend decomposing each sub-FR to the next level to define specific design parameters for redundancy failover, QoS prioritization, and network monitoring.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator control commands with reliable delivery to the remote vehicle.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit vehicle telemetry and sensor data to the operator with reliable delivery.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss, latency) to the operator and safety systems.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement error recovery and retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact.

assumptions: Customer-defined packet loss threshold (≤1%) and network conditions are formally documented and stable.
Dual network paths (e.g., cellular + satellite) are available at the worksite.
Teleoperation module has sufficient engineering capacity to implement layered resilience architecture.
Network Link interface can be abstracted to support multiple network technologies without Teleoperation rework.
Safety-critical control data can be isolated from best-effort telemetry data with separate SLAs.
open_risks: Redundant channels increase system complexity and cost; cost-benefit trade-off must be validated with customer.
Adaptive QoS and dynamic bandwidth allocation introduce non-deterministic behavior; safety-critical control paths must be isolated.
Network monitoring overhead may consume bandwidth and worsen packet loss if not carefully bounded.
Failover latency (switching between channels) may exceed operator tolerance; requires testing and tuning.
SafetyAlert delivery must be guaranteed even under communication degradation; out-of-band channel may be required.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of customer-defined packet loss threshold and network conditions.
Network topology and availability of dual communication paths at representative worksites.
Operator tolerance for failover latency and communication quality degradation.
Cost-benefit analysis of redundant channels vs. single-path QoS optimization.
Formal verification or testing evidence that DP-D achieves ≤1% packet loss under agreed conditions.
Interface contracts for Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link with explicit SLAs.
